Opus 4.7 dropped a few days ago and half the internet is treating it like some massive breakthrough.

It isn't.

It's a marginal step up over 4.6, where some benchmarks move 3-4 points, one or two actually regress, and the agentic coding numbers got a visible haircut for safety reasons.

AI hasn't had a real zero-to-one moment since GPT-3 dropped in 2020. That was the last actual jump, where a capability that genuinely didn't exist before suddenly existed. You could type a sentence in English and get working bash out the other side. Every model release since then has been a horizontal expansion of that same capability, dressed up as a paradigm change.

The AI you're building with today is the same category as the AI from 2022—it just needs less scaffolding. Back then you had to write custom harnesses, use highly specific prompts, and eat a lot of retries to get anything usable. Now you raw-dog a prompt and get a decent answer 80% of the time.

Which means AI doesn't make new things possible anymore; it just makes more things profitable. Personalized outreach at scale, financial analysis at speed, content ops that used to need a team. None of this is new…it's finally cheap enough to ship at a margin.

Every time a model drops, people rip up their infrastructure to chase a 3-point benchmark gain. They switch from 4.6 to GPT 5.4, then back, then over to whatever launched on Tuesday, and each swap is weeks of replumbing. Meanwhile, the compounding winners picked a stack a year ago and spent that year building distribution and better scaffolding on top of it, which is worth more than any benchmark delta.

Pick a model that works for you, build around it, and go sell something.

I did a full Q&A video a while back covering the most common questions I get from people starting out.

Three of them flip the standard advice on its head hard enough that I want to talk about them here.

Should you niche down hard, or go broad?

The usual take is pick one niche and go deep. For a total beginner, I think that's wrong. You don't know enough about your market yet to pick the right niche on the first try, and if you miscalibrate, you waste 90 days finding out. The better move is to pick three adjacent niches and run outreach into all of them for 90 days simultaneously. The work isn't 3x; it's closer to 1.5x because the services you'd sell to a SaaS company overlap heavily with those you'd sell to an agency or an MSP. You get 3x the shots on goal for 50% more effort. At the end of the 90 days, drop the two weakest and double down on the winner.

When do you stop outreach?

Short version: you don't. Lead gen is the answer to almost every business problem you'll run into. Not enough leads? Lead gen. Too many leads? More lead gen, because what that really means is you're accepting every client who shows up instead of picking the highest-paying ones. Overwhelmed with projects? Same answer. When you're in scarcity, you take the $500 client because it's what's in front of you. When you have 50 leads in your pipeline, you take the $10K one and skip the rest. The perceived problem is capacity. The real problem is almost always pipeline depth.

How do you work faster and stop overplanning?

Feedback beats planning, every single time. Imagine sailing from New York to Portugal. If you stand on the dock plotting the perfect bearing before you leave, you're going to miss by 500km because the weather, the currents, and the wind change faster than you can predict them. What works is setting off in roughly the right direction, reassessing often, correcting, and going again. Same thing with outreach, offers, and agency work. Ship the imperfect version, get real feedback from real prospects, and adjust. People who spend months planning the perfect first shot almost always lose to people who took 20 messy shots in the same window.

These three are the ones I'd build your first 90 days around. There are more Qs I answered covering the $100 starter stack, which automations are easiest to sell, who pays for tools (you or the client), and how to position yourself with zero experience.

Most of the AI automation content online is selling you tactics and acting like its strategy.

And tactics only have a shelf life of weeks, while strategy compounds for decades. If you only copy the surface layer, you're going to make some money for a minute and then watch it evaporate when the market saturates.

I've watched this cycle play out maybe a dozen times across different industries now: Web design in the early 2000s, then dropshipping, and finally social media marketing agencies.

Someone figures out a tactic, a guru packages it into a course, thousands of people flood in, and the opportunity is closed by the time you hear about it.

If you want to be a consultant first, an automation builder second then you need to learn how small to mid-size businesses operate, diagnose where revenue is being left on the table, and use automation as one of several possible ways to deliver.

This skill set survives every tactical collapse, because you're selling business understanding.

Automation is just the current best delivery mechanism. In five years, it might be something else, and if you built your moat on the tool, you'll be starting over. If the diagnostic skill is your foundation, then you just swap in the new tool and keep going.

Anthropic dropped Claude Routines last week, and I think it's the first time we've seen a 1-to-1 replacement for n8n, Make, and every other no-code automation platform people have been building businesses on top of for the last three years.

I've made a few "n8n is over" style videos before, and I'll admit most of them were premature.

Every previous contender replaced some of the surface area but missed a piece, whether it was scheduling, webhook triggers, API firing, connector breadth, or the orchestration layer in the middle. They were close, but Routines are the full overlap. Schedule, webhook, API call, GitHub event, a growing connector library, natural language prompting in place of drag-and-drop nodes, and it runs on Anthropic's infrastructure so your machine doesn't have to be on.

The old automation pattern was a three-part chain: 

1. You had an event on the front (schedule, webhook, whatever)
2. The middle was a bunch of drag-and-drop logic you built in n8n with credentials, auth, variable mapping, and field manipulation
3. And the output went off to Slack or your CRM or a database. 

That middle chunk was where most of the labor lived and where most of the value was captured, because building it correctly took skill.

Routines replace the middle entirely with a prompt. What used to take me two and a half hours to build out as a proposal generator in n8n, I one-shotted as a Routine in about two minutes.

I'm not saying everyone using n8n needs to rip everything up tomorrow.

If you're running flows that work, leave them alone. Tokens cost more than compute, and porting stable infrastructure for the sake of porting it is how people waste weeks. But if you're starting something new today, Routines are where I'd build it.

And if you've been sitting on automation ideas you didn't build because the no-code lift was too heavy to justify the payoff, those are worth another look. The build cost just dropped by an order of magnitude.

I've sold several million dollars worth of AI products and services over the last couple years, and I can tell you that most of the approaches I see people taking online are total dog water.

First, AI doesn't make things “possible”—it makes things profitable. Every business I've scaled with AI was built on a process that already worked. At my copywriting company, we scaled to just under $100K/month. All AI did was let each writer produce more, faster, which meant clients paid us faster and we operated with lower headcount than our competitors. The margin improvement was massive, but the underlying business model was the same one that existed before AI.

If you want the straight line path to making money with AI, stop trying to build novel AI products. Start looking for pre-existing profitable processes and just make them better. That's it.

The second thing people don’t talk about much is where AI falls apart. AI is incredible in high-volume, low average order value businesses. It's a terrible fit for low-volume, high-ticket environments. If you're selling a $25K product and AI reduces your conversion rate by even 5% compared to a human setter, you're losing $125,000 a month in opportunity cost. That dwarfs whatever you'd save on payroll. I had someone ask me about using AI to qualify private equity deals—we're talking potential $10M transactions. If AI reduces the probability of closing by 1%, that's $100,000 gone every single inquiry.

Meanwhile, if you're in a high-volume business where you'd need 10 or 15 setters to handle the flow, replacing that with AI even at a slightly lower conversion rate can absolutely work because the personnel savings are enormous.

The third thing, and I think this is the most important one: If you can type "make me a Netflix clone" into a prompt window and get a working product 10 seconds later, then the product is no longer the moat.

Because of AI, the only defensible strategy any business has going forward is distribution.

When my partner and I started scaling LeftClick, I was convinced our problem was that we needed more leads. We had a healthy pipeline, deals were coming in, but growth was stalling and I couldn't figure out why.

Turns out the bottleneck wasn't at the front of our business at all. 

We were taking on custom automation projects that required so much hands-on work that we physically couldn't push more clients through the system. Didn't matter how many leads we generated—they'd just pile up and stall.

Once we identified that and fundamentally changed what we sold (we productized), our close rate doubled and we scaled past $70K/month with one VA.

This is a framework called the theory of constraints, and it's one of my favorite topics in business because it explains why so many people feel busy all day yet their bank accounts stay empty. The answer is almost always that they're optimizing the wrong thing.

Every business is a pipeline. 

Stuff comes in on the left, money comes out on the right. And just like water in a pipe, your total output is always limited by the narrowest section. If your bottleneck is in fulfillment and you keep dumping more leads into the front end, you're just flooding the system and creating more work in progress without making any more money.

The framework has five steps: 

1. Identify the constraint
2. Exploit it (squeeze every drop of efficiency out before spending money)
3. Subordinate everything else to it
4. Elevate it (now you can hire or buy tools)
5. Then repeat because fixing one bottleneck always reveals the next one

The golden rule is you exploit before you elevate: Hire last, not first. 

Most agencies do this completely backwards…they find a bottleneck and immediately throw people or money at it, which just scales the inefficiency.

I broke this down in a video a while back with real examples from LeftClick and from members inside Maker School. Carousel below has the framework if you want the quick version.

I've sold the exact same system for $500 and $5,000. What changed was my ability to deliver value.

First, I started solving problems that cost more money. I'd get on a call with a marketing agency charging $10K/month and discover they had two needs.

Expense reporting—admin staff manually submitting receipts, accounting team spending 3-4 hours a week categorizing them. That costs them maybe $1,000 a month. Then speed to lead—they're responding to inbound inquiries in 3 hours instead of 5 minutes, and five leads a month are leaking through the funnel. At their conversion rate and contract value, that costs them $30,000 a month. Both take me 3-4 hours to solve with a Make.com workflow. Both use a webhook trigger, some data storage, and a transformation step.

In general, the closer your solution is to revenue generation, the more people will be willing to pay for it.

Second, I stopped just giving people the system and started giving them an experience.

Think about coffee. You can make a cup at home for 30 cents or buy the same cup at a cafe for $6. What's the difference? Certainly not 20x worth of beans and water. It's the smells, the barista, the interior, the Wi-Fi, and the energy. That cafe increased the perceived value by 20x by changing everything about the coffee except the coffee itself.

So instead of just delivering a speed-to-lead system for $3,000, I deliver the same system plus a full environment config, weekly strategy calls, Slack access, Loom documentation, seven additional automation templates, CRM integrations, and a 90-day optimization SOP. I charge $9,000. The extra work is maybe 5 hours on top of the 10 I was already spending. The client thinks they're getting a whole agency relationship.

Third, I just started charging more.

When I was starting LeftClick, I would routinely see people sell the same systems I was selling for $700 for 10 grand. And I would think to myself, how the hell can they sell such a simple system for that much money while I'm here slaving away for a fifteenth of that?

So I took my $1,500 projects and priced them at $4,000. I did not change a single thing about the service—I just got a completely different base of clients. These guys had money and things to do, so incentives insisted they trust my process and give me as much control as possible. Way fewer fixed costs, way less of a pain to deal with, and significantly more upsell opportunity later. You can get away with charging a lot more than you probably think. Most people never stress test it.

I used to spend 5 to 10 hours onboarding every new client at my cold email agency. Now it takes about 90 seconds.

I tell my agentic workflow: "Hey, new client, onboard them." It pulls the sales call transcript, generates a full proposal with personalized problem statements and ROI math, sends a three-part welcome email sequence from different team members, scrapes and enriches leads with the right filters, casualizes every company name so the outreach doesn't read like spam, writes three split-tested cold email campaigns based on our highest performers, sets up an automated reply system with a knowledge base for each campaign, and uploads everything to Instantly ready to send.

All I have to do is a quick QA pass at the end—checking copy, previewing emails, and adjusting any spacing issues.

The way this works is a framework I call DOE—Directive, Orchestration, Execution. You store high-level instructions in plain language files, which are basically your SOPs. You give the agent access to execution scripts it builds itself. And then the orchestration layer figures out how to chain everything together. I don't know how to read most of this code. I don't worry about most of this code. That's the AI's job. The AI is much better at coding than I would ever be, even given a decade of learning.

What's really cool is when something breaks, the workflow just self-heals. During one run, it hit a deprecated API key, found a working one stored somewhere else in my system, updated its own documentation to reference the correct key, and kept going. If I were running a procedural workflow on Make.com or n8n, that would have just errored out. This thing is like Wolverine—it gets shot and the skin comes back.

If you want to do this for your own business, the process is straightforward. Start by compiling your SOPs in natural language. Write them so a monkey could understand them. Send each one to the agent and test it once. AI is only going to get this right maybe 75% of the time the first run, and that's okay. Second time it's 97%. Third time it's 99%. Rinse and repeat across every SOP in your business, then create one meta directive that chains them all together top to bottom.

I’m giving away the exact process I used to hit $72K selling automation services.

The problem isn't that most people can't build automations—it's that automation is expensive and the deliverables are hard to explain, so getting your foot in the door is brutal.

That's where no-brainer offers come in. A no-brainer offer is a simple, productized deliverable you can fulfill quickly, at low cost, that demonstrates value and naturally leads into your more expensive services.

Three requirements for an offer to qualify:

You can do it quickly. Under one week turnaround, minimal client involvement, a few scheduled progress updates to give the impression of momentum.

You can do it cheaply. If you're solo, something you can fulfill in under 2 hours. If you're delegating, under $200. Both are possible if you're using templates.

There's little or no risk for the client. This is the biggest lever most people ignore. I think of conversion rate as (cost x ease x desire) / risk. You can't eliminate risk entirely, but you can mitigate most of it.

Real example from my business: "I'll build you a world-class cold email outreach system in 14 days for $1,325. We source 5,000 prospects, write your campaigns, and you get positive responses directly in your inbox. If you don't get at least 20 leads in two months, you get all your money back."

That cheapest templated lead gen system took us 45 minutes to fulfill and cost us under $50.

The real money isn't in the no-brainer offer though—it's in the upsell. If your offer logically leads into a recurring retainer, 5 clients a month at $3K with a 6-month average lifespan is $90K from the same number of customers.

My Claude Code skills work about 70% of the time. The other 30% is a bag of rocks.

I wanted to fix that without sitting there rewriting prompts by hand, so I took a concept from Andrej Karpathy's new autoresearch repo and applied it to my own skill library.

The idea is simple. You define a few binary yes/no evaluation criteria for your skill, then you let an agent run the skill dozens of times, score every output, tweak the prompt, and keep the winner.

I tested it on my diagram generator. Four criteria: Is all the text legible? Does it match my pastel color palette? Is the layout linear? Is it free of unnecessary numbering? Max score of 40.

The agent generated 10 diagrams every 2 minutes, evaluated all 10, mutated the prompt, ran again.

Started at 32 out of 40. Hit 39 out of 40 within a handful of runs. That's 97.5%. Total cost was about $10.

One thing I learned fast: keep your evals binary. Yes/no, pass/fail. The moment you introduce scoring scales, you compound variability at every step and your results get noisy. And don't make criteria so narrow the model starts gaming the test instead of actually improving the output. That's the equivalent of a student who doesn't understand the material but still aces the exam.

If you have skills that work most of the time but not all of the time, this is how you close that gap.

Andrej Karpathy just dropped an open source repo called autoresearch that when you combine it with something like Claude Code becomes self-improving AI.

Karpathy is widely regarded as one of the foremost voices in machine learning. He was a founding member at OpenAI and later head of AI at Tesla. So when he releases something, I pay attention.

The idea is pretty elegant: You give an AI agent a small but real setup and let it experiment autonomously overnight. It modifies something, runs a test, checks if the results improved, keeps or discards the change, and repeats. You wake up in the morning to a log of experiments and hopefully a better outcome than what you started with.

In his case, he was training a machine learning model. The agent would adjust hyperparameters, train for 5 minutes, measure validation loss, and loop. After just a few runs his model got significantly better without him doing anything.

Now I'm not in machine learning. I take models other people have made and use them to make money. But the moment this dropped, I started thinking about ways to apply the same principle to my own business.

To make a long story short, the requirement is simple. You need an objective metric you can track, an API you can call to measure it, and something the agent can modify between runs. If you have those three things, you can build an autonomous experimentation pipeline for almost anything.

I've already set this up for my cold email campaigns. Reply rate is the metric, the Instantly API is the measurement tool, and the agent iterates on the email copy. It runs every hour via GitHub Actions. I'm not even in the loop anymore.

I'm also running it on my Claude Code skills and I used it to take an old web app from 1,100ms load time down to 67ms across 67 autonomous runs.

Landing pages, ad creatives, product descriptions, YouTube titles, pricing pages, chatbot scripts. If you can measure it and an agent can change it, autoresearch applies.

The way it works is you set a campaign on a clipping platform that says something like "if you get me 1,000 views, I'll pay you $2." But the economics are interesting—most of these platforms have view thresholds where the clipper has to hit a certain number of views before they get paid out at all. If they don't hit it, you don't pay. So your effective cost per view ends up being dramatically lower than what you'd expect on paper.

You combine both of these, and you have near-zero production cost with near-zero distribution cost.

Anthropic is running their $200/month consumer plan as a loss leader. OpenAI is spending faster than they're earning.

Meta dropped a hundred million on talent acquisitions alone.

None of these companies are optimizing for profitability. They're all making the same bet that AGI is close enough to justify burning cash at this rate.

And it's not just the labs—governments are starting to push this too. When you look at how much is being spent on AI infrastructure and how quickly policy is shifting to accommodate it, none of it makes sense unless you assume massive productivity gains from machine intelligence are right around the corner. If that doesn't materialize, it can get ugly pretty fast.

The optimistic version of this story is that AGI arrives, productivity explodes, and everyone benefits.

But the question I keep turning over is what happens if AGI does arrive and the gains concentrate at the top. Because right now, most of the economy isn't building AI businesses.

Most people are doing bookkeeping, spreadsheets, service work, stuff that's increasingly automatable. If 70-80% of the workforce can't meaningfully compete with AI agents even after upskilling, you end up with unemployment levels that historically have led to very bad outcomes.

The Arab Spring kicked off with about 25% youth unemployment in Tunisia. Support for the Nazi party in Germany tracked almost one-to-one with unemployment. When people can't feed their families, they stop caring about ideology and start caring about whoever promises bread.

I'm not saying that's where we're headed. I genuinely don't know.

This could be 5 years away, could be 20, could never happen. But I do think the people who are building and deploying AI systems right now are in the best position regardless.

If you're the one controlling the agents, you capture a share of the upside no matter which version of the future we end up in.

Anthropic ran a study where they split programmers into two groups. One used AI, one didn't.

The AI group scored 17% lower on a quiz about the code they just wrote. That's the equivalent of two letter grades.

The kicker was AI only saved them about 2 minutes on the actual task, and that difference wasn't even statistically significant. So they learned less and didn't meaningfully speed up.

I think about this in terms of horizontal versus vertical growth.

Horizontal growth is when you take something that already works and multiply it. You figured out how to make soap, so you buy more factories.

Vertical growth is when you come up with an entirely new method of making soap that lets one factory do the work of a thousand.

AI is incredible at horizontal growth. You can produce more code, more writing, more applications, more websites. But all of that comes at the cost of vertical growth, which is your ability to actually innovate and solve new problems from scratch. And vertical growth is the hard stuff. It happens in bursts.

The situation where AI makes the most sense is when you already have a strong foundation. You've built the skill, you understand the fundamentals, and now you're just trying to produce more of what you already know how to do. That's where the leverage is massive.

But if you're trying to learn something new and you hand the hard parts off to Claude, you're basically asking your gym buddy to lift the weight for you and then wondering why your arms aren't growing.

AI is going to be better than us at basically every economically valuable skill. That's not a question of if, it's a question of when.

So if you build your entire identity around producing economically valuable output, you're going to have a rough time when a model can do it faster and cheaper. What AI can't replace yet: your relationships, your critical thinking, your ability to make decisions under uncertainty.

Outsource the economic part if it makes sense. Don't outsource the you part.

Most people lose their cold email leads after the prospect already said yes.

Someone replies "yeah, let's book a call" and then the sender writes back "great, let me know what time works for you." Now they're just sitting there waiting. The prospect gets busy, forgets, moves on.

Every additional message you send after that first positive reply will necessarily reduce the total number of people who actually end up on a call with you. Think of it like a sieve—the more steps you add, the more people fall through. So the goal is to compress everything into as few steps as humanly possible.

The moment someone says yes, I assume the booking. I pick a time, I book them in, and I send them both an email reply and a calendar notification simultaneously. Something like: "Hey Peter, thanks so much for getting back to me. I'll book you in for Thursday at 10am—you'll get a calendar notification in a minute. If anything changes, would you mind sending me your calendar as well? Linking mine below for convenience. Look forward to chatting."

What happens when someone gets double-notified with both an email and a calendar invite for a specific time? One of two things. Either they show up, or they respond to reschedule. Both of those outcomes are infinitely better than "let me know what time works" followed by silence.

I also link my own calendar in case the time genuinely doesn't work for them, so they can rebook without having to send me another message. And I ask for their calendar too because it's useful to have for the future. The whole point is minimizing the number of back-and-forth messages required to get from "yes" to "on a call."

On top of that, there are two automations I consider non-negotiable for anyone running cold email. The first sends a follow-up one day before the meeting: something casual like "Hey Sam, really looking forward to our chat tomorrow. I'll be a couple minutes early in case you have the time. No pressure." The second sends two hours before, just a quick human-sounding note confirming you'll be there on time. Both of these need to feel like a real person wrote them rather than a generic template. 

Set those two up and your show rate goes up by probably 10-20%, which is enormous when you consider how much money you're already spending on leads and outreach to get someone to say yes in the first place.

AI right now is like a video game character with every point dumped into intelligence at the expense of everything else.

Pick an average human off the street, and their skill distribution is a balanced hexagon: decent at coding, okay at writing, and adequate at reasoning and humor. AI's distribution looks nothing like that. It's wildly spiky, absolutely crushing coding and reasoning while lagging behind on things like humor, empathy, and general relatability.

This is why there's such a massive disconnect in how people evaluate these models. Someone asks GPT to write the funniest joke ever, gets a mediocre result, and concludes the whole thing is overhyped. Meanwhile, the people actually building these models are measuring coding and reasoning benchmarks, which is why they're convinced we're approaching something massive.

The more useful exercise is to average all of it out. Take the highest peaks and the lowest troughs and find the middle. When you do that, current AI models land roughly on par with human intelligence. Comparable to a very specialized person who's exceptional in a few areas at the expense of others, but human-level nonetheless.

And that spiky shape is filling out faster than most people realize. Every generation of models, the goalposts move. Six or seven years ago, the consensus was that AI could generate abstract squiggles but would never produce coherent art. Now we have photorealistic image generation that's basically indistinguishable from photography.

When GPT-3 came out, the pushback was that it would never replace 15 years of cultivated SQL expertise. Within a couple years, it was outperforming the median programmer at database queries. The current line in the sand is "it can only reproduce things it was trained on, it can't do anything truly new."

That line will move too.

These models have the potential to deliver genuine abundance and solve problems that have plagued humanity for as long as we've been around. But the window to position yourself on the right side of this shift is narrowing, and the spiky shape is filling out whether we're paying attention or not.

Every couple of weeks, Boris Cherny (the creator of Claude Code) drops what I call a biblical thread on X, breaking down how he uses it to maximize effectiveness. When the person who built the tool is handing you their personal playbook, you pay attention.

The issue is that a lot of his tips are hard to interpret without practical context. Here are the ones that made the biggest difference for me.

1) Git worktrees: 

Instead of building features sequentially, you create separate branches and run multiple Claude Code instances across them in parallel. Three branches running simultaneously means three features being developed at once, merged back together when they're done. Realistic speed improvement of 3-5x depending on how many branches you run.

2) Plan mode: 

Research occurs 10-100x faster than implementation. Editing a plan to perfection before writing a single line of code will save hours of revision downstream. Same principle as scoping a project with a client: clarify everything upfront instead of building first and fixing later.

3) Iterating your CLAUDE . md: 

Every time Claude makes a mistake, tell it: "Add this to CLAUDE . md so that a future version with no context could one-shot what we just did." Most people update their CLAUDE . md manually or don't update it at all. This one habit compounds massively because you're training your environment to never repeat the same mistake twice.

4) The "scrap and rebuild" prompt: 

After a messy build, tell Claude: "Knowing everything you know now, scrap this and implement the more elegant solution." Claude searches through multiple potential approaches when it builds, and your repo ends up cluttered with the debris of failed attempts. One prompt cleans it all up.

5) Sub-agents for code review: 

Spin up a sub-agent with zero context from the original build to review your code objectively after every major write. No prior decisions polluting the evaluation.

6) Skills as GitHub repos: 

Turn your best SOPs into reusable skills and store them in a shared repo. Anyone on your team can pull specific skills into their own projects as needed.

7) Ghost TTY: 

The terminal manager Anthropic's own team uses daily. Faster, less compute-intensive, and built for running multiple Claude Code instances efficiently through keyboard shortcuts rather than mouse navigation.

P.S. I use Claude Code every day across a business that does over $3M a year in profit and teach over 2,000 people inside Maker School how to put tools like this to work. If you want to learn how to actually leverage Claude Code in your business, that's what we focus on.

Imagine you’re a hunter-gatherer on the plains 20,000 years ago. A time machine opens up, somebody grabs you, and drops you into a modern office cubicle. Someone explains that this is how people get paid now.

You'd look around and think these people are out of their minds. Where's the hunting? Where's the gathering? Why is everyone sitting down for 8 hours? Nobody here is doing anything actually valuable.

What we consider "work" has been completely redefined multiple times throughout human history. Every single time, the people living in the previous paradigm looked at the new one and thought it was insane.

I think we're about to go through that again.

The United States announced about 110,000 layoffs for January with only around 5,000 new jobs planned. That's a ratio of less than 1 in 20 new jobs created to old jobs lost—the highest number of January job losses going all the way back to 2009.

What makes this different from the industrial revolution is that AI has replaced the task and its oversight. When factories replaced handwoven baskets, the basket weaver became the factory overseer. There was always a next rung on the ladder to step up to. With AI, it's better at the task, better at overseeing the task, and better at orchestrating the oversight of the task. At a certain point, you have to ask where the economic value of the human actually is.

The conclusion I keep coming back to is that at some point, compensation has to be divorced from direct economic productivity. And it’s not because that's ideologically appealing—my parents fled the Soviet bloc, so I'm not sitting here glorifying alternative economic systems.

But in a world where you have $100 to spend and an AI can give you 1,000 products at the same quality level that a human can give you one, who are you going to pay? 

The entire economy is built on a consumption loop: people work, earn money, spend money, businesses hire, and the cycle continues. When you remove the "people work" part of that loop, everything downstream breaks.

I don't know exactly what the next version of work looks like. But if you grabbed someone from today and dropped them into a post-AGI economy, I guarantee they'd think it was just as insane as the hunter-gatherer thought about the cubicle.

Somewhere between a few hundred and a few thousand Clawdbot instances have been hacked.

Services like Shodan constantly scrape every available URL on the internet and index them into a database.

When you run Clawdbot on a VPS, you post it to a publicly available URL. And if you search one of these databases for "Clawdbot control"—which appears on every Clawdbot control page—you get a list of thousands of servers you can access by literally clicking a button.

It gets worse.

If these servers use EngineX as a reverse proxy, due to a quirk in how EngineX interfaces with Clawdbot, you get full access to anyone's control panel. Messages sent to and from that person. All their skills. All their config information. All their API keys.

Most people using Clawdbot upload all their API keys, tokens, and secrets to their messaging platforms and services. With that access, you have someone's whole entire life.

A white hat hacker named Jameson went through, found someone's information, fully identified them, and pulled their entire message history. He also built a simulated backdoor skill on Claude Hub, inflated its download count to over 4,000, and got it highlighted on the front page.

This is the red wedding of vibe coding happening in real time.

I really like technology like this. But right now, I wouldn't trust all my API keys with Clawdbot.

The point of a sales call is to transfer trust.

Get the person on the other end to trust you and the rest takes care of itself.

Connor Kaplan broke this down in a Maker School session. Three things he focuses on:

Conviction. He asks himself before every call—would I actually buy this? Not "is this a good product" or "can I help this person." Would I actually pull out my wallet and buy this thing? If the answer is wishy-washy, the prospect will feel it. They always do. You can't fake conviction. You either have it or you don't, and if you don't, you need to figure out why before you get on another call.

Confidence. Connor does pushups before his calls. Not joking. 5-10 reps, gets his energy up, walks into the call in a different state than if he'd just been sitting at his desk scrolling Slack. He also pulled every question he's been asked on sales calls, wrote out answers, and practiced them 15 minutes a day for two weeks straight. Now he doesn't get caught off guard. Someone throws an objection at him and he's already got the answer loaded. That kind of preparation looks like natural confidence on the call.

Consistency. Sales is a volume game and most people's energy craters after a few bad calls in a row. Connor keeps a swipe file of clips from his best calls. When he's in a funk, he watches them. Reminds himself what good looks like. He also actually uses the "next step date" field in his CRM so people don't fall through the cracks. Sounds basic but almost nobody does it. They let leads go cold because they forgot to follow up. Connor doesn't forget.

I put the Whimsical board Connor made in the comments if you want the full breakdown.

Hormozi banned questions in his community. If you ask a question, you're out. If you pitch something—also out.

I watched the Skool Games replay and it's the complete opposite of how I run Maker School.

When someone joins my community, I want them to feel like they can ask anything. Post your dumb question. Someone will help. That's the whole point.

But it feels like while Hormozi's running a gladiator pit, I'm running a co-op.

And his method is working: the engagement is high, people are posting constantly, and the results speak for themselves.

So I sat with it for a while and tried to figure out what's actually going on.

And I keep coming back to one thing: Hormozi could've set *any* rules and people would follow them. He's the co-owner of Skool. He's got the $100M brand. He's been building credibility for over a decade. If he said "you can only post on Tuesdays" or "every post needs a haiku," people would do it.

So is the no-questions rule actually the strategy? Or is it just him?

I don't think you can separate the two.

My takeaway is this: The value of a community is its members helping each other. Hormozi's rules force that. You can't ask—you can only give. So people give.

I do the same thing differently. I set the culture as "help each other" and then I actually help people publicly so they see what it looks like. Then they copy it.

There's no single way to run a community. You pick your rules. You enforce them. And if you're consistent, people fall in line.

Here’s the reason AI can’t handle everything end-to-end yet: your workflow with 5 steps and “90% accuracy” isn’t actually 90% accurate. 0.9 × 0.9 × 0.9 × 0.9 × 0.9 = 59%. You run it 1,000 times and you’re getting 410 failures.

Now imagine you're sending invoices—you get one wrong 5% of the time…that doesn't cost you 5% of your business, it costs you the client.

This is the problem with letting AI handle everything end-to-end.

LLMs are probabilistic. They predict distributions, not certainties, so every step you ask them to handle adds variance to the chain.

But I use a framework that *massively* improves outputs and accuracy:

• Directives → plain English SOPs. What you want done.
• Orchestration → the AI layer. Reads directives, makes decisions, adapts when things break.
• Execution → deterministic scripts. One job each. No variance. No guessing.

AI handles judgment. Code handles repetition.

That's the DOE Framework.

Deterministic code does the same thing every time, while also being 10,000x faster and basically free.

My revenue scaling journey:

• $0-$25K/mo → Upwork alone
• $25K-$75K/mo → cold email
• $75K-$125K/mo → YouTube
• $125K-$400K/mo → community, partnerships & acquisitions

What got you here won’t get you there: every approach has a ceiling. If you want to get to $1M/mo, don’t just plan to send more Upwork applications because you’ll need a different approach.

Your path won’t be exactly like mine, but here’s how I think about it:

1/ $0-$10K per month use Upwork

Send proposals every day—it’s the fastest way to land clients.

But you’ll quickly learn that Upwork isn’t the best way to scale because you have to do custom work for every client.

2/ $10K-$50K per month use cold email

You can productize your offer and sell the same thing to lots of new people.

But you’ll quickly learn you become the bottleneck to growing the business because you’re so busy fulfilling for clients (some of you will cap at $25K/mo and others at $70K/mo).

3/ $50K-$100K+ per month use leverage

Think content, products, communities, or bigger teams.

The goal is to find something with a step-change function in output. For me, that was YouTube + Maker School. For you it might also be content + community, or it might be hiring some automation experts and taking on more clients.

Business isn’t a game of linear improvements.

Your income goes up with one approach, then plateaus—and you have to fundamentally change your strategy to break through to the next level.

I asked 1,000+ AI agency owners how they’re signing clients. Here’s what actually works in 2026

1. Upwork (Most Popular for Beginners)

“It’s a freelancing platform and you can’t scale.” Sure. But you don’t need $1M/year. You just need some clients.

Some key notes:

• Beginners suck at sales. Eliminates need to identify problems or explain solutions (clients already know both)
• 30% proposal-to-reply rate possible with quality applications (avg is ~20%)
• $4,300 closed in first 12 days by one member
• Strategy: start with smaller projects to build reviews, then scale pricing

2. Cold Email (Highest ROI)

Highest ROI of all the methods. Also quite scalable. This is the #2 most popular client acquisition method. 

• Lots of initial effort setting up campaigns but most scalable method once set up (just respond to leads)
• Get lead source (like apollo), scrape it (apify), enrich it (leadmagic), feed to AI
• Set up mailboxes (google) and campaigns (instantly)
• Benchmark: 2-3% positive response rates.

3. Communities (Best for Building Authority)

Find niche communities. Add value. Be “the guy” in that community. Skool groups, FB groups, etc.

• Member signed 4 clients in one month sharing demos in Facebook groups
• Takes patience: 2-3 months to build authority
• Higher close rates due to trust factor (40-50%)
• Perfect if you're starting with no budget. Just takes work. Scales with your time and you need to show up every day

4. LinkedIn/Social Media

Not great as the primary approach. But great as a complementary strategy. Very scalable but not as direct a return.

• Lead magnets get a bunch of comments
• Long-term ROI but requires patience. Can take months to work
• Focus on problem-solving content.
• Fewer people win. But when you do win, you win really big

5. YouTube (High Long-term Value)

This has worked great for me. Show what you do with long-form videos, then use the description link for a form.

• Member landed $3,000 client directly from YouTube
• 3-6 months to hit "product-content fit". High long-term ROI, but significant upfront time investment
• Tutorial-style content works best
• High conversion despite low view counts

6. Social Media DMs (Another Cold Outreach Channel)

X/LinkedIn makes a lot of sense for B2B. Voice and audio messages work well. So do Loom videos.

• Overlooked but effective
• Use the "do you know anyone who..." approach

A few takeaways:

• Start with direct methods (Upwork, cold email) before content strategies
• Go to warm leads (people you already know) for the straightest line path to getting clients

Once you’re getting clients, then start worrying about making it “scalable”.

3 reasons why you should start an agency instead of a SaaS:

1. More upfront revenue

• If it’s your first time running a business, know this: agencies make 10-20 times the upfront revenue as compared to SaaS companies.
• You can make $100K in a couple of mo with an agency—whereas the vast majority of SaaS companies will take years to get to that point, even with a ton of outside capital.
• Better to make a fuck ton of money quickly, and then use that to pour gasoline on the fire of a SaaS company later
• Lifestyle benefits—you’re not just eating soup ramen all day and worrying about rent, you have enough of a base to think long term and strategize about what you really want

2. You learn business fundamentals

• Agencies are usually about providing an *ongoing* *service.* Ongoing services force you to learn client management. Client management is one of the most essential skills in business—your ability to set expectations, identify points of value, and deliver above & beyond apply to all business verticals including SaaS later
• You also learn how to manage a team. Similar idea—SaaS founders who can’t manage teams almost never break past $10K/mo, so if you want to do this learn early

3. You’re forced to be scrappy and bootstrap, which is the single most important mindset to have in a cash flow positive business

• Agencies don’t raise money, so they have to watch every penny they spend extremely closely.
• SaaS companies often have to go through multiple financing rounds in order to succeed. They’re also net unprofitable for the first few years on average. Fundraising is a very different skill than acquiring customers.
• This is fine if you want to make a unicorn, but most people that get into business do so because they merely want to be wealthier than 99% of their peers while working <10% of the time. Lifestyle is the guiding north star for most of you and financing rounds is not the way you achieve it.

You’re call but I know what I’d pick if I were starting out.

After working with 100+ businesses and hiring 10+ employees, these are some of the most common hiring mistakes I see:

1/ Not tracking revenue per employee (RPE)

Most 7-8 figure agencies I work with never track this explicitly. It's one of the most important metrics in your business.

Calculate it now: Annual revenue ÷ employee count

Before your next hire, know exactly how much revenue you expect them to generate. If you're hiring an account manager for $5K/month, define their target revenue contribution upfront and track it religiously.

2/ Not having daily accountability

I've seen people making $10K/month spend 3/4 of their day doing absolutely nothing.

It's not because they're bad people—humans naturally economize energy when accountability is absent. Here’s the fix: Every position needs daily duties with simple tracking. A one-question Typeform. A spreadsheet. Evidence their work is going somewhere.
The simple act of monitoring is usually enough.

Awareness solves a lot of problems.

3/ Hiring as the first solution

Before you hire, spend one hour exploring alternatives. Pretend you can't hire anyone—what would you do?

Often you'll find you can automate the work, redistribute among current staff, or use a productized service for a fraction of the cost. Hiring should be your *last* solution. Not your first one.

You want a simple, productized deliverable you can fulfill for the client:

• At low cost
• While demonstrating value

You just want an accountability partner.

Someone who will ask if you sent your Upwork proposals and cold DMs. If you followed up with prospects. If you’re making sales or wasting time on random tasks in your business.

Meanwhile, an accountability partner can be free. Or you can join a community like Maker School, full of people with similar goals: without giving up 50% of your business.

OR another thing that happens with wanting a partner: it’s a guise. You say internally, and sometimes subconsciously “I want to avoid the hard work. Sub $50k/mo businesses rarely need strategic partners—they need operators.

When I started my first business doing door-to-door, I didn’t get a partner because I didn’t know what to do. I did it so after getting the door slammed in my face 100x that I’d have a shoulder to cry on (sounds dramatic, but really). Otherwise, I probably wouldn’t have lasted after the first day.

If you acknowledge you’re getting a business partner—just for the support—then great. As long as you know why you’re doing it.

Went on quite the walk last week and had some thoughts:

1. Despite making more money than the majority of the world's inhabitants, I still have next to no physical presence. The scale of capital is mind-boggling.

Example: downtown Calgary is littered with large office buildings that the city walks by ever day. Renting even a single office floor plate in one of those towers costs ~$1M/yr, a sizeable portion of my annual revenue.

I thought I was making a lot of money until I realized this! Our physical world is *constructed* and *shaped* by overwhelming amounts of money. The average person can never hope to achieve that, let alone understand it.

If I find the annual lease cost of a floor plate mind-boggling, imagine Joe Blow who makes just ~$50,000/yr.

2. Agencies are a great way to bootstrap initial revenue. Then, info is a great way of taking those learnings + cashflow, and turning it into a much more scalable generator of income (top 0.01%).

That's what I'm doing. But even then, despite everything going right... I am still making, objectively, very little money.

What is ~$5M/yr in the face of the sort of capital that buys whole office towers? Even a single one would cost tens, hundreds of millions—over a decade of forcing earnings in the top 0.01% of my country.

Of course, the idea is not to use your own money at scales like this. You borrow, leverage debt, etc (multiply your capital with risk). But it is still instructive as an exercise. Even the most scalable information product will not easily make you a billionaire—see Hormozi and his recent $100M book launch (many years of his life for just 1/10th of a billion).

3. The digital world is so much more impactful per dollar spent if you're optimizing for recognition. I didn't fully realize how much until now.

Instead of buying a building for $100M—something that would exist in the consciousness of every person in my city who walks by it every morning—I could buy "digital real estate", i.e banner ads that follow a similar number of people around on the internet. I could do this for YEARS at one one-hundredth of the sticker price.

Alternatively, I could invest a tenth of *that* into some sort of organic marketing team, apply a bit of my time to it, and then have several football stadiums of business owners listen to me talk about whatever I want every couple of days. Probably much more impactful.

Anyways. Not all of these thoughts make sense, but I know they are leading somewhere. I am excited and glad to have the opportunity to continue doing what I am currently doing, but the scales of real money still seem out of reach.

I wonder what the straightest line path to achieving "building money" is.

Just landed your first automation agency sales call? Great. Here's my 7-step checklist for nailing that call and closing the deal:

1. Skip the hard sell. Your job is to consult and understand.

2. Ask these key questions:
• "What made you want to [book this call/post on Upwork/respond to my email]?"

4. Provide a high-level solution: "Here's how I would fix it: [brief explanation]"

5. Confirm fit: "Given everything you've told me, this makes sense and I can help."

6. Set next steps: "I'll send you a proposal with more details."

7. Offer a follow-up: "We can set up another call to walk through it if needed."

Remember: You're not selling automation. You're selling peace of mind and efficiency.

This framework has helped me close a lot of clients. Use it, tweak it, make it your own.

Most importantly: Practice. Every call makes you better.

Here’s the thing that a lot of people get wrong right off the bat with proposals: they think it’s about proposing a solution.

It’s not.

A proposal is a test. The client wants to see: do you clearly understand my problem and can you articulate it back to me? If the answer is yes, they will hire you. If it’s no, they won’t.

Here’s what I recommend your proposal look like:

• 50-60% describing the problem
• 20-30% describing the solution
• 10-20% covering the legalese, payment terms, etc.

Start it like this:

• “Your team is heavily misallocating their time and it’s costing you $20K/mo. Here are the three reasons why.”
• “Lack of structured management is costing you Y dollars per month. Here is why.”
• “Hesitance to restructure your business is costing you Z dollar per month. Here is why.”
• “Your bottleneck is thing and it is costing you X amount. Here is why.”
• “By not solving thing, you will continue to feel pain and it will eventually result in X outcome. Here is why.”

Describe the problem.

Then wrap up with a brief solution.

Once you show you understand the problem better than anyone else, you win the job.
